questions.text: What file defines a Helm chart's metadata (name, version, description)?
questions.explanation: Chart.yaml is the required metadata file for every Helm chart. It must contain at minimum: apiVersion, name, and version. It also holds description, appVersion (the app version, not the chart version), and dependency declarations.

questions.text: How do you override a value at install time without modifying values.yaml?
questions.explanation: The --set flag overrides individual values at install or upgrade time. For multiple values, use --set a=1,b=2 or multiple --set flags. For complex overrides, use -f custom-values.yaml. --set takes precedence over values.yaml.

questions.text: What is the purpose of _helpers.tpl in a Helm chart?

options.text: Stores default values for the chart
Contains named templates (partials) reused across other templates

questions.explanation: _helpers.tpl (note the leading underscore) is a conventions file for defining named templates using {{- define "name" -}}...{{- end -}}. Files with _ prefix are not rendered as manifests. These helpers are called with {{ include "name" . }} in other templates.

questions.text: Which command rolls back a Helm release to a previous revision?
questions.explanation: helm rollback <release> <revision> rolls back a release to the specified revision number. Use 'helm history <release>' to see revision numbers. Helm stores release history as Kubernetes Secrets in the same namespace.

questions.text: What does the | nindent 4 pipeline do in a Helm template?

options.text: Adds a newline then indents all lines by 4 spaces

questions.explanation: nindent N adds a leading newline and then indents every line of the multi-line string by N spaces. This is used when including multi-line template output into YAML that requires specific indentation. Regular indent N does not add the leading newline.

questions.text: How do you add a chart dependency (e.g., a PostgreSQL subchart)?

options.text: Add it to Chart.yaml 'dependencies' then run helm dependency update
Manually copy the chart into the charts/ directory
Use helm install for each dependency separately

questions.explanation: Chart dependencies are declared in Chart.yaml under 'dependencies' with name, version, and repository. Running 'helm dependency update' downloads them into the charts/ directory. Alternatively, you can manually place charts in charts/. Dependencies can also be conditioned with the 'condition' field.

questions.text: What is the difference between chart 'version' and 'appVersion' in Chart.yaml?

options.text: They are the same — both track the application version
'version' is the chart package version; 'appVersion' is the version of the app being packaged

questions.explanation: Chart 'version' (SemVer) tracks the chart itself — it increments when chart templates or values change. 'appVersion' tracks the application version (e.g., the Docker image tag). They are independent. Helm uses 'version' for packaging; 'appVersion' is informational.

questions.text: How does 'helm upgrade --install' differ from 'helm install'?

options.text: upgrade --install installs if the release doesn't exist, upgrades if it does

questions.explanation: helm upgrade --install is idempotent: it installs a new release if it doesn't exist, or upgrades an existing one. This is ideal for CI/CD pipelines where you don't want to branch on whether the release is new or existing.

questions.text: Where does Helm store release state in Kubernetes?

options.text: In a file on the control plane node
In ConfigMaps or Secrets in the release namespace
In etcd directly via a CRD

questions.explanation: By default (Helm 3), release state is stored as Secrets (type: helm.sh/release.v1) in the same namespace as the release. Each revision is a separate Secret. This can be changed to use ConfigMaps via the HELM_DRIVER environment variable.

options.text: Installs the chart in a test namespace
Validates chart structure and template syntax without rendering
Checks the chart against the Kubernetes API server

questions.explanation: helm lint checks a chart for possible issues: incorrect chart structure, invalid YAML, missing required fields in Chart.yaml, and other best-practice violations. It does not connect to a cluster. Use helm template to render and then kubectl --dry-run=client for server-side validation.
